chunk_summary: Guidelines for confirming and managing blood pressure in children with obesity, including proper measurement techniques, use of ambulatory BP monitoring (ABPM), and treatment thresholds based on pediatric hypertension guidelines.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of accurate blood pressure measurement in children with obesity.
Learn when to use ambulatory blood pressure monitoring (ABPM) to confirm hypertension.
Identify treatment thresholds and referral criteria for pediatric hypertension.
Recognize high-risk phenotypes requiring specialized management.
key_facts: Single BP readings are often inaccurate; confirmation requires proper technique and repeat measurements across visits.
Ambulatory BP monitoring (ABPM) is recommended for persistent elevated BP, stage 1 hypertension across three visits, or suspected white-coat/masked hypertension.
Start antihypertensive medication if hypertension persists after lifestyle changes, is symptomatic, or coexists with diabetes or chronic kidney disease.
Follow-up intervals: 3–6 months for lifestyle management, 4–6 weeks for medication titration until target BP is achieved.
Refer to pediatric nephrology or cardiology for stage 2 hypertension, suspected secondary causes, or target organ damage.
reference_quote: "The AAP 2017 pediatric hypertension guideline emphasizes ABPM to confirm hypertension and to reduce misclassification from white-coat or masked hypertension." (AAP Clinical Practice Guideline, 2017)
document_key_topics: Blood pressure measurement techniques in children with obesity
Use of ambulatory blood pressure monitoring (ABPM)
Treatment thresholds and referral criteria for pediatric hypertension
